mnemonics.title: Structure Declaration Syntax
mnemonics.content: Remember as: 'struct TAG { TYPE member; }; VAR;' - TAG names the structure, BRACES hold members, SEMICOLON ends declaration, VAR creates the variable.

mnemonics.title: Dot vs Arrow Operator
mnemonics.content: Use DOT (.) when you have a VARIABLE (name.member), use ARROW (->) when you have a POINTER (ptr->member). Think: 'Arrow for Address'.

mnemonics.title: Structure vs Array
mnemonics.content: STRUCT = Different TYPES (S-T), ARRAY = Same TYPE (A-T). S-TORE heterogeneous, A-LIKE homogeneous.

mnemonics.title: Typedef Benefit
mnemonics.content: typedef makes 'struct' optional. Without typedef: 'struct Point p1;' With typedef: 'Point p1;' - Less typing, cleaner code.

acronyms.expansion: STandard or Special Type that Holds Unified Collection of data Types
acronyms.usage: Remember: structures unify different data types into one composite type
A structure without special member functions (in C++). Simple data containers like int, float, char members.

acronyms.expansion: Size Of Structure Includes Data + Extra Bytes for Alignment + Padding
acronyms.usage: When calculating structure size, remember: sum of members + padding bytes = total size

visualTips.visualization: Draw a structure like a table: each row is a member with its type and name. Show contiguous boxes representing bytes. Add hatched boxes for padding. Example: |char a|xxx|int b| = 1+3+4 = 8 bytes
Think of nested structures like folders in a file system. To access innermost member: parent.child.grandchild. Use dots like: p.birthDate.day
Draw a variable box with a dot on it. Draw a pointer box with an arrow pointing to the structure. DOT for variables, ARROW for pointers.

analogies.analogy: A structure is like an architectural blueprint for a building. The blueprint (struct definition) describes what rooms and features exist, while actual buildings (variables) are built from that blueprint.
Think of a structure as a paper form/record card. A student ID card has fields: Name (text), Age (number), Grade (letter). Each field is a different type, like a struct holds different data types.
Arrays are like identical mailboxes in an apartment complex (all same type, indexed). Structures are like filled-out forms with different types of information in each section.
Structure padding is like packing items in a suitcase with dividers. CPUs access data faster when it's aligned, so 'empty' bytes (padding) are inserted between items for proper alignment.
